content:
8 min read
1 hour ago

--

This is the script for my ITP 2026 thesis presentation.
I begin with Recho, my thesis project that was accepted as a poster at CHI 2026, and end with a question I’ve been thinking about lately: Why do we still need to code in the age of AI?
The presentation is about 10 minutes long. Feel free to watch the recording here!
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Who am I
Hello everyone. I’m Bairui Su. It’s so great to see all of you here! Today, I’m going to talk about Coding on Principle. Sounds a little abstract? Don’t worry, let’s get started with a simple question:
Who am I?
Well, I’m the…
early thesis finisher.
For this year, the most common feedback I received was: “Oh, you’ve already done everything. Did you play video games today?” yes I did. But did I finish my thesis, I’m not entirely sure. To explain why, let’s go back a little bit.
What’s the problem?
I’m a toolmaker, who has always been interested in making coding more accessible and hopefully, more playful. These interests led me to where I am now — pursuing a master’s degree at NYU ITP.
Before coming to NYU, I spent 3 years building tools for information visualization. The highlight of this journey was leading the development of AntV G2 5.0, a free, open-source JavaScript library for interactive data visualization.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Through those experiences, I gradually realized one thing:
Well designed library doesn’t necessarily make coding more accessible, the environment does.
A good example is p5.js. While p5.js is popular for its beginner-friendly APIs, it’s the p5.js web editor that truly makes p5.js more accessible. This makes me wonder: can we have a similar environment that keeps the accessibility and playfulness of the p5.js web editor, but focuses more on the computational processes?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

or perhaps realizing my collaborator Luyu Cheng’s vision?
We want to live in editor forever.
Introducing Recho
To explore these questions, we’re building Recho: A reactive programming interface for learning algorithms and ASCII art.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Recho’s core is built on a statement by Bret Victor:
Programming is blindly manipulating symbols.
By “blindly”, he means we can’t easily see the results of our manipulations. This is why programming is so difficult.
For example, this is a piece of code. It’s not easy to tell what this program does at a glance, because we don’t know what each line is doing exactly.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

With Recho, the situation is different. You can see results directly in the editor. The program becomes less “mysterious” as you observe how it works. Clean, split, filter and count. You quickly realize this is a word-counting program.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Word Counting Example in Recho
As you may have noticed, Recho provides a built-in function called echo. This is the most important part of Recho: it evaluates expressions and displays the output directly in the code editor as comments.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here is a simple example showing how Echo works.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

echo in Recho
With the help of Echo, not only does programming becomes less invisible, it also allows you to do something creative:
Like a running animation:

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Author Animation in Recho
Press enter or click to view image in full size

火炎焱燚 (Fire!)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Moondial: Moon Sundial
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Maze
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Number Input in Recho
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Donut in Recho, Ported from Alex Nak
Not enough? The code for the donut is also a donut.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is Recho. Recho is not trying to replace existing programming interfaces like Jupyter notebooks or the console. It just wants to introduce:
A lighter way to code with creativity.
By “lighter,” we mean you can start with a simple idea, write simple code, and get simple results. By “creativity,” we mean you can end up make something fun and discover new ways to express yourself or understand the world.
The Process
All of these wonderful things started with an inspiring talk with my collaborator, Luyu Chen. He showed me something he was working, and it suddenly clicked.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

After that, I built the proof of concept of Recho in two days, then kept polishing it by posting stories to the instagram…
Press enter or click to view image in full size

until we had workshop to released it and open sourced it a month later. Thanks to the open source club and Mumu for hosting this!
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The following month, I performed with Recho Melody, another programming interface, at NIME. I hope you guys enjoyed it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Next, I invited people to try Recho Multiples at the ITP Winter Show. Thanks everyone who created their trees.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This February, Recho was accepted as a poster for CHI 2026, one of the top academic conferences in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

And we were lucky to travel to Barcelona to present it few weeks ago. That’s pretty much everything about Recho.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What happened in 2026?
You may have noticed that all the important work was done in 2025.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

So what happened in 2026? What stopped me from continuing to work on it? The answer is
AI
At the end of 2025, everyone was talking about Claude Code and how people could quickly make software and digital art, even without coding. My social media was full of those cool projects. It was exciting, but it also made me feel terrible. If nobody cares about code anymore, why build a coding environment for learning? Why write code at all? For the first time, I felt completely lost.
Then I got rejected by the MIT Media Lab, which made that period even darker. To get through it, I slowed down and started to review my previous works. I tried to find the answer that mattered even more than Recho:
What’s my coding principle in the era of AI?
My Coding Principle
And I realized something very important.
Coding is the most effective way of learning.
I learned inverse kinematics by code, created this smiling robot arm, Frankenstein.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

FrankensteinI learned music by code, created Chart Music Lab, exploring the relationship between charts and sounds.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Chart Music LabI learned light by code, created M2, animating static images by Malus’ Law of polarization.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

M2I learned typography by code, created APack, a writing system that writes alphabets in the style of Chinese characters.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

APackI learned nature by code, created Name2Tree, a system transforms your name into a unique tree. People tried it in the last ITP 2025 Spring show.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Name2TreeAfter that I planted all the trees into a infinite landscape, learned the essence of Chinese painting, “A Thousand Li of Rivers and Mountains” by Ximeng Wang.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Infinite LandscapeLast ITP Winter Show, Chloe and I printed it out, learned how to use Riso printer.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I learned time by code, created soft time and the Chinese polar clock to explore color and ancient Chinese timekeeping system with Julia Xu.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Soft Time (Left) and Chinese Polar Clock (Right)
I learned US cities by code, created U.S. Map Paper, exploring a new way of map visualization.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

U.S. Map PaperAs you can tell, code has always been
the most flexible medium for me to learn.
It lets me experiment quickly, make ideas concrete, and understand a concept by building a working version of it. AI can help with many parts of the process, but it cannot replace the moment when I truly understand something because I struggled with the logic, made a mistake, and fixed it myself.
As long as I remain curious about the world, I will keep coding without AI when I really want to learn something.
Since I’m a toolmaker, I’ll put it another way:
Toolmaking is the most effective way of learning.
That’s my coding principle.
Acknowledgment
At last, I want to thanks all the people who are supportive during this whole journey.
Thank my family. Their love and support are the reason I am able to stand here today.
Thank my professors: Daniel Shiffman, Tanika Williams, David Stein, Gottfried, Noah, Karl, Yeseul, Lusia, Alison and many others. They have guided me, challenged me, and helped shape who I am becoming.
Thank all my friends: Sai, Jin, Jack, Arjun, Julia, Chloe, Luyu and so many others. You made this journey joyful, unforgettable, and full of warmth.
Two years went by so fast. I still remember the very first day, sitting there and watching everyone share one thing about themselves. And now, it’s time to say goodbye.
I’d rather think of it as a celebration — to celebrate the time we spent here, to celebrate the people we met here, and to celebrate that we are ready to apply what we learned here, and what we survived here, to our next destination. With that, good morning…
Oh and In case I don’t see ya: good afternoon, good evening, and good night.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Thank you all for watching!
